Carveyance · Engineering FEATURE / 16 MIN READ

Tall, Short,
High, Low

A 4.10 gear is called low. A 2.73 is called high. The bigger number is the lower gear, the lower number is the taller gear, and on a bicycle every one of those words means something else again. The confusion is not your fault — but the fix is a single sentence, and once you have it, everything from towing to 35-inch tyres falls into place.

By Carveyance Editorial · Part two of two — following One-Wheel Drive

Short / numerically high
4.10:1
Driveshaft turns 4.1 times per wheel turn. Leverage, revs, thirst
Tall / numerically low
2.73:1
Fewer input turns per wheel turn. Calm, quiet, economical, lazy
The hidden third variable
Your tyres
Fit 35s to a truck built for 32s and you re-geared it without touching the axle
The Setup

Four Names
For One Thing

Walk into any group of car people and ask whether a 4.10 gear is a high gear or a low gear. You will get four answers, at least two of which will be delivered with total confidence, and at least one of those will be wrong.

Here is the mess in full. A 4.10 is a numerically high ratio. It is also called a low gear. It is also called a short gear, and sometimes a deep gear. Meanwhile a 2.73 is numerically low, and is called a high gear, and a tall gear.

So the higher number is the lower gear. The lower number is the higher gear. Both statements are correct and they sound like a joke.

Now add bicycles, where the same words got attached to the opposite hardware. On a bike, "low gear" means the big sprocket at the back — the one you use to crawl up a hill. On a car, "low gear" means the big number. Those two facts feel contradictory to anyone who has ever ridden a bicycle and then read a truck forum.

People fake their way through this for entire careers. They know that trucks that tow want "4.10s" and that highway cars want "3.23s," and they repeat it correctly without ever being sure why, in the same way you can use a word properly for thirty years without being able to define it.

The confusion exists because everyone is arguing about what to call the number, and almost nobody says what the number is counting.

So let's say what it's counting. It takes one sentence, and it dissolves the whole problem.

I · The One Sentence

It's a Count of
Revolutions

A gear ratio of 4.10:1 means this and nothing else: the driveshaft turns 4.10 times for every single turn of the wheel.

That's it. It is not a rating, not a grade, not a quality score. It is a count. Input revolutions per output revolution.

And it comes from somewhere completely physical — the number of teeth on two gears. The big ring gear in your axle has a tooth count. The little pinion that drives it has a tooth count. Divide the first by the second and you have your ratio.

Which brings us to the most satisfying object in this whole story — because on a real ring gear, somebody stamped the answer into the metal at the factory.

Forty-one divided by eleven is 3.727. Round it and you get 3.73 — one of the most common axle ratios ever fitted to an American car. The number in the catalogue is not marketing. It's arithmetic that was performed by a gear cutter decades ago.

# The whole thing, once
ratio = ring gear teeth ÷ pinion teeth

41 ÷ 11 = 3.73 — highway-friendly
41 ÷ 10 = 4.10 — towing and big tyres
41 ÷ 15 = 2.73 — economy cruiser

Notice what's happening. To make the ratio numerically bigger, you make the pinion smaller. Fewer teeth on the driving gear means it has to spin more times to push the ring gear around once. More input turns per output turn. A bigger number.

II · Why It Matters

You Are Buying
a Lever

Counting revolutions would be trivia if it didn't come with a consequence. It does, and the consequence is the entire point.

Every gear reduction is a lever. Give up distance, gain force. It is the oldest trade in mechanics and it has never once been renegotiated.

Spin the input four times to turn the output once, and the output receives roughly four times the torque — minus a few percent lost to friction, which is why axles get hot. You have not created energy. You have spread the same energy over more input revolutions and collected it as leverage.

Animation 01

4.10:1 — the pinion turns four times for one turn of the ring
Tooth counts are drawn to scale — both gears use identical tooth spacing, which is why the small one has ten and the large one forty-one. Watch the orange marker on the ring gear complete one revolution while the pinion completes four.

Now the same drawing with a tall gear, so you can see the trade run the other way.

Animation 02

2.73:1 — a bigger pinion, fewer input turns, less leverage
Same ring gear, larger pinion. The wheel now turns once for only 2.73 input revolutions — so at any given engine speed the vehicle travels further, and the axle multiplies torque less.

That is the entire economy of the thing. There is no ratio that gives you both. Every gearing decision ever made is a position on one line, with pulling power at one end and relaxed high-speed cruising at the other.

III · The Resolution

Your Legs Already
Know This

The fastest way to fix the vocabulary problem is to stop reading and go look at a bicycle, because a bicycle is a gearbox with the cover removed and you have already felt every ratio on it through your own thighs.

Put the chain on the largest rear sprocket and you can climb a wall. Your legs spin quickly, the bike moves slowly, and the pedals feel light because the gearing is multiplying your effort. That's a short gear. Numerically high. Low gear.

Put the chain on the smallest rear sprocket and you can descend at thirty miles an hour, but starting from a stop is agony because each pedal stroke is trying to move the whole bike a very long way. That's a tall gear. Numerically low. High gear.

Identical physics, identical trade, and no confusion whatsoever, because you're feeling it instead of reading about it.

Why the words got crossed "Tall" and "short" describe the gear's behaviour — how far the vehicle travels per input revolution. "High" and "low" originally described the position of the lever in an old gearbox: you shifted up into high gear, down into low. And the numerical ratio counts input revolutions. Three different naming systems, invented by three different groups of people, all still in use. They aren't contradicting each other. They're describing different things and nobody labelled the axes.

The whole vocabulary, in one table

What you're describingThe torque endThe speed end
Example ratio4.10:1   4.56:1   4.88:13.08:1   2.73:1
NumericallyHigher numberLower number
CalledShort, deep, lowTall, high
Input turns per wheel turnMoreFewer
Torque at the wheelMoreLess
Engine rpm at 70 mphHigherLower
Fuel economyWorseBetter
On a bicycleBig rear sprocketSmall rear sprocket
Good forTowing, big tyres, acceleration, crawlingHighway cruising, economy, quiet, top speed

Read down either column and every term agrees with every other term. The apparent contradiction only appears when you jump between columns mid-sentence — which is precisely what people do when they say a truck has "good tall gearing for those big tyres," meaning the opposite of what they said.

IV · The Bill

What Leverage
Costs at 70 MPH

Gearing is not free, and the invoice arrives on the motorway. Because ratio fixes the relationship between wheel speed and engine speed, choosing your leverage also chooses how hard the engine is working every mile you drive.

The arithmetic is simple enough to do on a napkin:

# engine rpm at a given road speed
rpm = ( mph × ratio × 336 ) ÷ tyre diameter in inches

# 336 = (5,280 ft × 12 in) ÷ (60 min × π)

The highway penalty

Engine rpm at 70 mph on a 30-inch tyre, by axle ratio
Calculated from the standard formula above. Direct drive assumes a 1:1 top gear, which isolates the axle ratio. With overdrive applies a 0.70:1 top gear, typical of a modern automatic. This gap is the single most important development in gearing of the last thirty years — see below.

Look at the two rows and you have the whole modern story. In direct drive, a 4.10 axle spins nearly 3,200 rpm at 70 mph, which is loud, thirsty and unpleasant. That's why 1970s muscle cars with short gears were miserable on a road trip — you could have acceleration or you could have a cruise, and the gearbox gave you no way to have both.

Add a 0.70 overdrive and that same 4.10 axle drops to about 2,250 rpm. You kept the leverage for starting, towing and climbing, and handed the highway problem to the transmission.

Eight, nine and ten-speed gearboxes exist to end the argument this article is about. You no longer have to choose.

Which is why a modern half-ton truck can ship with a 3.73 or even 4.10 axle and still return respectable highway economy, while its 1985 equivalent on 3.08s pulled badly and drank fuel. More ratios means the compromise gets subdivided until it nearly disappears.

What it looks like from the driver's seat

This is also why "what rpm should I cruise at" has no universal answer. The number your engine shows at 70 mph is not a fact about the engine. It is a fact about the ratio between it and the road — the gearbox, the axle, and the tyres, multiplied together.

Which brings us to the variable almost nobody accounts for.

V · The Hidden Multiplier

Your Tyres Are
a Gear Ratio

Here is the part that costs people real money, and it is the reason this article exists as a companion to the last one.

The final ratio in your drivetrain is not in the axle. It's bolted to the outside of it, it's made of rubber, and you change it every few years without thinking of it as a drivetrain decision at all.

A wheel is a lever too. Its radius is the lever arm. Make the tyre bigger and the same axle torque now acts through a longer arm, which means less force at the contact patch — and simultaneously the vehicle travels further per wheel revolution. That is precisely, mathematically, the definition of a taller gear.

Run the consequence. Your truck left the factory with a 3.73 axle on 31.9-inch tyres. You fit 35s because they look correct and you want the clearance. You have now, without opening a single housing, converted that 3.73 into an effective 3.40.

# what your gearing actually became
effective ratio = old ratio × ( old tyre diameter ÷ new tyre diameter )

3.73 × ( 31.9 ÷ 35 ) = 3.40
3.73 × ( 31.9 ÷ 37 ) = 3.22

# and to get your original gearing back:
3.73 × ( 35 ÷ 31.9 ) = 4.09 → order 4.10s

The gearing you didn't know you changed

A factory 3.73 axle, as tyre diameter grows
Effective ratio calculated as original ratio × (original diameter ÷ new diameter), starting from a 31.9-inch factory tyre. Revolutions per mile calculated as 63,360 ÷ (π × diameter). Real tyres often measure slightly under their nominal size, so verify with your actual mounted diameter.

This is the complete explanation for a complaint you have heard a hundred times: I put 35s on my Wrangler and now it's gutless and it hunts for gears on every hill. Nothing broke. The owner re-geared the vehicle taller by nine per cent, added rotating mass and rolling resistance on top, and then asked the same engine to deal with it.

8.7%
Effective gearing lost going from 31.9-inch to 35-inch tyres
3.73 → 3.40
What that does to a factory axle ratio, without touching the axle
4.10
The ratio you'd fit to get your original acceleration back
It also makes your speedometer lie Your speedo counts wheel revolutions and multiplies by a figure the factory chose for the factory tyre. Fit tyres 8.7% larger and every revolution now covers 8.7% more ground than the car believes. At an indicated 70 mph you are actually doing about 76. Your odometer under-reads by the same proportion, which quietly stretches your service intervals and flatters your calculated fuel economy. Most vehicles can be recalibrated; many owners never bother, and then wonder why the numbers never quite add up.
VI · Stacking It Up

Nothing Works Alone

The axle ratio gets all the attention because it's the one printed on a sticker, but it is one link in a chain, and the links multiply.

# total reduction, engine to pavement
transmission gear × transfer case × axle ratio = overall ratio

# a Wrangler Rubicon in first, low range:
4.71 × 4.00 × 4.10 = 77.2 : 1

# the same truck in top gear, high range:
0.67 × 1.00 × 4.10 = 2.75 : 1

That first number — the crawl ratio — is why a properly geared off-roader can idle over an obstacle at walking pace without touching the throttle. Seventy-seven engine revolutions for one turn of the wheel is an enormous mechanical advantage, and it's assembled from three modest reductions multiplied together.

The second number is the same vehicle, ten seconds later, on the road home.

One drivetrain, spanning a 28-fold range of gearing, because reductions stack. This is also the honest answer to "should I regear?" — often the better question is whether your transmission already has a ratio that solves it, because an extra gear in the box is cheaper than a weekend with the axles apart.

Choosing, honestly

If you mostlyYou wantBecause
Commute on highwaysTaller — 3.08 to 3.42Lower cruising rpm, better economy, less noise. You rarely need the leverage
Tow regularlyShorter — 3.73 to 4.10Keeps the engine near its torque peak on grades; less gear hunting; less transmission heat
Run oversized tyresShorter, to compensateYou already went taller by fitting the tyres. This restores what you had
Crawl off-roadShortest — 4.56, 4.88 and upMultiplied by low range for control at walking pace without slipping the clutch
Drag raceShort — 4.10 and beyondAcceleration is torque at the wheel. Top speed is irrelevant over a quarter mile
Drive a modern 8–10 speedWorry about it lessThe gearbox spans a wide enough range to cover most of the compromise for you
Regearing is not a bolt-on Changing an axle ratio means new ring and pinion sets, and setting them up correctly is genuine skilled work — pinion depth, backlash, bearing preload, and reading the contact pattern on the tooth face. Done badly it howls, runs hot and fails early. Both axles must match on a four-wheel-drive vehicle. Budget for a specialist, expect it to cost more than the parts, and treat anyone who quotes it as a quick job with suspicion.
VII · The Ending

And Then It
Stopped Mattering

Everything above exists because of one limitation of the internal combustion engine: it makes usable torque across a narrow band of engine speeds and essentially none at zero.

An engine at rest produces nothing. It has to be spinning, in a specific range, before it's any use — which is why cars need clutches, torque converters, multiple transmission ratios and a final drive, all conspiring to keep the engine in its happy band while the wheels do whatever the road demands.

An electric motor makes peak torque from zero rpm and pulls cleanly to speeds an engine cannot survive.

So the multi-speed gearbox largely disappears. Most EVs use a single fixed reduction, and the ratio you'd have argued about on a forum becomes an engineering decision made once, in a room you'll never visit, that you cannot change and have no reason to.

The lever didn't go away — you still need reduction between a fast-spinning motor and a slow-turning wheel. What went away is the compromise. There is no longer a trade between the ratio that launches well and the ratio that cruises quietly, because the motor is happy at both ends.

A century of gearing was an elaborate apology for the engine's narrow powerband. Remove the apology and you remove the argument.

Which is worth a moment of respect on the way past. That stamped ring gear at the top of this article — 11 and 41, struck into steel in 1966 — is a solution to a problem, executed in metal, permanent and legible sixty years later. Somebody chose those tooth counts deliberately, knowing exactly what they were buying and exactly what they were giving up.

You can still read the decision. It's right there on the rim.

Four point one zero turns of the shaft. One turn of the wheel.

Everything else — tall, short, high, low, and every argument anyone has ever had about which is which — is just people disagreeing about what to call that.

Carveyance
Engine speed calculated as (mph × ratio × 336) ÷ tyre diameter in inches, where 336 derives from (5,280 × 12) ÷ (60 × π). Effective ratio after a tyre change calculated as original ratio × (original diameter ÷ new diameter). Revolutions per mile calculated as 63,360 ÷ (π × diameter); figures shown match the manufacturer comparison chart reproduced above. Crawl ratio example uses published Jeep Wrangler Rubicon figures — 4.71:1 first gear, 4.00:1 Rock-Trac transfer case, 4.10:1 axles. Nominal tyre sizes frequently differ from measured diameter; verify against your own mounted tyres before ordering gears. Overdrive figures assume a representative 0.70:1 top gear and vary by transmission.
